critique.summary: The article provides a detailed overview of the new features and changes introduced in Java 26, including updates to garbage collection, HTTP/3 support, concurrency improvements, lazy constants, and pattern matching enhancements. It also covers deprecations and preparations for future Java projects like Project Valhalla.
mainPoints: Java 26 introduces new HotSpot JVM features such as ahead-of-time object caching compatible with any garbage collector (JEP 516) and improved G1 garbage collector throughput by reducing synchronization (JEP 522).
The HTTP Client API now supports HTTP/3 (JEP 517), allowing faster and more reliable HTTP connections with fallback mechanisms to HTTP/2 or HTTP/1.1.
Structured concurrency is enhanced (JEP 525) to provide better task hierarchy, cancellation, and error propagation, making concurrent programming more intuitive.
Lazy constants (JEP 526) are introduced to allow deferred, thread-safe initialization of immutable objects, improving startup performance and concurrency safety.
Pattern matching for primitive types is previewed again (JEP 530), extending support for primitive types in instanceof and switch statements.
Several features are deprecated or restricted, including warnings on mutating final fields via reflection (JEP 500) to improve program correctness and JVM optimizations.
The Vector API (JEP 529) continues incubation, enabling efficient SIMD vector computations in Java.
Most new features are in preview or incubator stages, requiring explicit enabling via command-line flags.
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately describes the features and JEPs included in Java 26, consistent with official OpenJDK JEP documentation and reputable Java community sources. The technical explanations align with known Java development practices and recent JDK release notes. The preview and incubator statuses of features are correctly noted, and the descriptions of improvements and deprecations match official JEP proposals. The article does not make unsupported or false claims and provides code examples and rationale consistent with Java's evolution.
considerations: The article does not mention the potential impact on existing Java applications when adopting preview features, which may require careful testing and gradual adoption.
It does not discuss the compatibility or migration challenges for developers moving from Java 25 to Java 26, especially regarding deprecated features and new command-line options.
There is no discussion of the broader ecosystem support for HTTP/3 or the adoption timeline for Project Valhalla features that Java 26 prepares for.
The article assumes familiarity with Java internals and JEPs, which may limit accessibility for less experienced developers.
Performance implications and memory overhead trade-offs of new garbage collector enhancements could be further detailed for practical decision-making.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the evolution and current state of Java, with several commenters reflecting on its improvements, cultural challenges, and ecosystem. Some express appreciation for recent language features and the removal of outdated components, while others lament the slow adoption in Android and the legacy issues.

comments.author: ludovicianul
comments.text: I program in Java for more than 15 years now. I can resonate with people hating the language from it's early days due to the experience with all the enterprisy features and over abstractions. Or confunding Java with the Spring ecosystem. But Java came a long way over the years. It's now what many would call a "modern" language. It's less verbose, has many of the features people find appealing in Scala and Kotlin and it can even compile to native binaries using GraalVM. This made building CLIs in Java feasible. Or lambdas.
comments.analysis: This comment provides an informed, experienced perspective on Java's evolution, accurately highlighting improvements and modern capabilities.

comments.author: xeubie
comments.text:
I think astronomers could measure the age of the universe in nano-Valhallas. Every year, it feels 50% closer to completion...

In all seriousness I'm happy with what Mr. Goetz and the team have done. Sealed interfaces (java 17) + exhaustive switch statements (java 21) means we now have union types in java! And instead of jumping on the async/await bandwagon we now have a more general solution that doesn't lead to API duplication (virtual threads). But Valhalla has been a veeery long time coming.
comments.analysis: The comment correctly identifies recent Java features and their significance, showing a good understanding of language design and concurrency improvements.

comments.author: vyskocilm
comments.text:
JEP 504: Remove the Applet API

Glad to see this being removed. Java plugins especially on Linux were awful and required by tons of corporate stuff. Anyone remeber IcedTea Web? A functional and opensource Java plugin and Java Webstart implementation?
comments.analysis: Accurately notes the removal of a deprecated and problematic API, reflecting a positive change in the Java ecosystem.

comments.author: dzonga
comments.text:
the people that work on Java & the JVM are very smart.

it has become a best of breed language - hell its better than Go for industry purposes.

the drawback with Java will always be the CULTURE - (maybe someone can insert a quote of how in physics progress is only made, when old physicist die - I don't wanna be morbid ) but with Java same that's when the culture will change.

All those people using typescript (could be using Java - but the culture doesn't want them and consider them heretics for not embracing religion of OOP and FactoryFactory)
comments.analysis: While the cultural critique is subjective, the comment insightfully points out that Java's community and culture can hinder adoption of new ideas.

comments.author: einrealist
comments.text: I have been using Java since version 1.4. Both the language and its ecosystem have come a long way since then. I endured the height of the EJB phase. I adopted Spring when version 1.2 was released. I spent hours fighting with IDEs to run OSGi bundles. I hated building UIs with Swing/AWT, many of which are still in use today and are gradually being replaced by lovely JavaFX. When I look at code I wrote around 12 years ago, I'm amazed at how much I've matured too.
comments.analysis: Provides a credible personal history that aligns with known Java ecosystem developments, adding valuable context.

comments.author: freedomben
comments.text: As someone who has been out of the Java world for many years, but recently forced back into it due to Android dev requirements, Post lawsuit, what is the relationship between Android (Google) and Java now?  When can we expect 26 on Android?  On that note, why is Android always so far behind?  Is it because Kotlin is primary or is it deeper?  Did the lawsuit play a role?
comments.analysis: Raises valid questions regarding Android's slower adoption of Java versions and the influence of Kotlin and legal history.

comments.author: haolez
comments.text: I was pretty surprised when I learned recently that the Java alternative for green threads doesn't use colored functions. It put Java in a higher place in my perception.
comments.analysis: Accurately notes a technical detail about Java's concurrency model, reflecting some understanding of implementation choices.

comments.author: OSaMaBiNLoGiN
comments.text:
All the changes look great. But I don't know how I feel about the syntax. A lot of things that very well could be first-class just aren't. Instead of a `lazy` keyword, we get `LazyConstant<T>`.

I'm sure there's reasons as to why. I just don't know them.
comments.analysis: Expresses a reasonable critique of syntax design, though lacks detailed knowledge of design rationale.

comments.author: oystersareyum
comments.text: Do changes to Java itself impact e.g. Clojure? I think it uses many java primitives instead of emitting jvm byte code but I can be wrong.
comments.analysis: Raises a relevant question about language interoperability on the JVM, though the technical detail about Clojure is somewhat imprecise.

comments.author: wiseowise
comments.text: Android as always in shambles. Shame that neither Google, nor ecosystem cares about Java because they’ve bought JetBrains kopium.
comments.analysis: The comment is emotionally charged and somewhat speculative but reflects common frustrations about Android's Java support.
